d. c. Coates, socialist candidate
for congress, IS too busy serving

mission that is writing the new
city charter to look after his con-
gressional race Yet from CORE
spondence received and personal as
surapces of support, Mr.. Coates be-
lieves that the socialist vote in the
Third congressional district will

Il had intended to make an active
campaign during the month proceed
ing the election said Mr. Coates,
in discussing his congressional can


didacy, but the work of trying to
secure decent charter for the PEG.
pie of this city has taken all of my
time. When man puts in full
day at manual or mental labor and
| then gives the night to the arduous
task of helping to draft a suitable


charter for city of 100000 people,


he does not have much time left for
politics.


I'm the election of a congressman
| the people have an opportunity to
express themselves directly on the
fundamental issues in our economic
and industrial system. So long as
the corporations own the natural
resources and the machinery of in,
dustry, present conditions will con-
tinue. The wage worker will be
